ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general curry rice entry; no specific ebi fry curry rice entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific ebi fry curry rice code. A0FJP (rice-based dish) used as the closest defensible category anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of ebi fry curry rice served in a Japanese canteen or restaurant context typically weighs approximately 400 g, comprising a mound of steamed white rice, Japanese curry sauce with vegetables, and two to three pieces of breaded deep-fried shrimp.

nutrient_source.title: 555D Ebi Fry Curry Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 555D Ebi Fry Curry Rice(D), cr_id G00010978. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB record and is set to -1. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat are not reported and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for ebi fry curry rice. GI estimated at 65 based on the analogous food 'white rice with curry sauce' from the University of Sydney GI Database (GI ~65 for Japanese-style curry rice, medium category). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (64.0 g for 400 g portion × 0.2519 = 64.0 g net carb available) / 100 = 41.6. This is an analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended for clinical use.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g with beneficial potassium counterbalance at 327 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: At 231 mg sodium per 100 g, a standard 400 g serving delivers approximately 924 mg sodium — within a single-meal budget of ~800–1000 mg for hypertensive individuals — but curry roux formulations vary widely in sodium content, so requesting a reduced-sodium roux and limiting portion size to 300 g is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (25.2 g/100 g) partially mitigated by dietary fibre 3.7 g/100 g; medium GI ~65
type2_diabetes.guidance: The 3.7 g dietary fibre per 100 g slows glucose absorption and partially offsets the medium GI of ~65, but a 400 g serving still delivers approximately 64 g available carbohydrate, which will significantly raise postprandial blood glucose; individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit the portion to 250–300 g and pair with a non-starchy vegetable side to reduce glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried shrimp component contributes saturated and potentially trans fats from frying oil; cholesterol reported as 0 mg/100 g in HPB source
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-fried ebi component introduces saturated fat from frying oil (exact saturated fat content not reported in the HPB source), and the breadcrumb coating adds refined carbohydrate; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, request oven-baked or air-fried shrimp where possible, and monitor cumulative saturated fat intake across the day.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The deep-fried ebi component generates pro-inflammatory AGEs and the frying oil may contribute trace trans fats; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request oven-baked or air-fried shrimp and monitor tolerance to nightshade vegetables (potato) in the curry sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ebi Fry Curry Rice (エビフライカレーライス) is a popular Japanese-style dish consisting of steamed white rice topped with a thick, mildly spiced Japanese curry sauce containing vegetables such as potato, carrot, and onion, accompanied by one or more pieces of ebi fry — whole shrimp coated in panko breadcrumbs and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The dish is a staple of Japanese yoshoku (Western-influenced Japanese cuisine) and is widely served in Japanese canteens, family restaurants, and food courts across Singapore and Japan. The curry sauce is typically prepared from a commercial roux block, giving it a smooth, slightly sweet, and mildly spiced flavour profile distinct from South Asian curries. The ebi fry component provides a contrasting crunchy texture against the soft rice and thick sauce. Nutritionally, the dish provides 164 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (6.5 g), low fat (4.21 g), and a meaningful carbohydrate content of 25.2 g moderated by 3.7 g dietary fibre. Sodium is 231 mg/100 g, and potassium is notably high at 327 mg/100 g. The dish is suitable for most healthy adults but requires portion control for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its cumulative carbohydrate and sodium load at typical serving sizes. The deep-fried shrimp component is a concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to AGE formation during frying.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for this exact dish (cr_id G00010978, confidence 0.95). The HPB record is an approved primary source for Singapore-served dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for Japanese-style curry rice and is clearly documented as such.
notes: saturated_fat_g set to -1: not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not available in the HPB source for this dish. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient detail beyond the reported panel not available in the HPB source for this dish as of 2026-04-16. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as reported by HPB; this is a confirmed value from the approved source. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on Japanese-style curry rice from the University of Sydney GI Database; direct GI measurement for this specific dish is not available. Glycemic load per serving calculated for a 400 g standard plate: (65 × 25.19 × 4.0) / 100 = 65.5 g carbohydrate in serving × GI / 100 = 41.6. The input health_suitability entry for rheumatoid_arthritis referenced trans fat at 0.1 g/100 g; this value is not present in the HPB source and has not been carried forward — trans_fat_g is set to -1 pending a verified source. The dish is served in Singapore under the 555D menu designation (D = dinner set).
